summary: Gruber highlights a test by Dean Murphy showing that Safari Content Blockers in iOS 9 dramatically improve page load times by blocking third-party scripts. Using iMore as an example, Murphy found 38 third-party scripts adding 9 seconds to load time. Gruber praises iMore's editorial content but condemns their bloated website, noting that a 537-word text article weighs 14 MB. He argues the advertising industry has become gluttonous and disrespectful of users' time, attention, and battery life. Apple's Safari Content Blockers represent a way for users to fight back against obnoxious JavaScript tracking. He contrasts iMore's bloat with his own article weighing just 125-175 KB.
key_points: iOS 9 Safari Content Blockers reduced iMore's homepage load time from 11 seconds to 2 seconds by blocking 38 third-party scripts
A 537-word text article on iMore weighs 14 MB, while Gruber's equivalent weighs 125-175 KB — a damning comparison
Third-party JavaScript trackers continue hitting the network for a full minute after page load, draining battery life
The problem isn't unique to iMore — 10+ MB pages and minute-long network activity are industry norms even on mobile
Apple's Content Blockers target obnoxious JavaScript rather than ads per se, positioning users to reclaim control
money_quotes: I love iMore. I think they're the best staff covering Apple today, and their content is great. But count me in with Nick Heer — their website is shit-ass.
The fact that these JavaScript trackers hit the network for a full-minute after the page has completely loaded is downright criminal.

Advertising should have minimal effect on page load times and device battery life. Advertising should be respectful of the user's time, attention, and battery life. The industry has gluttonously gone the other way.
iMore is not the exception — they're the norm.
10+ MB page sizes, minute-long network access, third-party networks tracking you across unrelated websites — those things are all par for the course today, even when serving pages to mobile devices.
With Safari Content Blockers, Apple is poised to allow users to fight back.
Apple has zeroed in on what we need: not a way to block ads per se, but a way to block obnoxious JavaScript code. A reckoning is coming.
key_insight: The web advertising industry's unchecked bloat — turning simple text articles into 14 MB downloads with minute-long tracking scripts — has become so abusive that Apple's Safari Content Blockers represent a necessary and overdue reckoning.
